1: The first day of the seventh month is a yôm tərûʿâ — a day of trumpet-sounding. This is Rosh Hashanah (New Year) in the later Jewish calendar, though the term itself does not appear in Numbers 29; the first-of-the-seventh-month designation is expanded in Leviticus 23:24 as a šabbāṭôn zikrôn tərûʿâ (a Sabbath of remembrance with trumpet-blowing). The seventh month is the most sacrifice-laden month of the calendar: Rosh Hashanah (1st), Yom Kippur (10th), and Sukkot (15th-22nd) are all contained within it.
2: The Rosh Hashanah offering: one bull, one ram, seven yearling lambs — a notably smaller sacrifice than the monthly new moon (two bulls). The reduction from the monthly new moon offering may reflect the new month's offering (v6: still required) being its own offering, with the holy assembly adding only the single-bull-ram-seven-lambs complement. The structural parallel to Yom Kippur (v8: same quantities) connects the New Year and the Day of Atonement as a unit — ten days of awe bracketed by matched offerings.
3: The grain offerings at the standard graduated scale: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths for the ram. The consistency of the grain-offering quantities across all major holy assemblies (Passover, Shavuot, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur) establishes these four as structurally equivalent in the sacrificial system, even though their ceremonies differ significantly.
4: One-tenth per lamb for the seven Rosh Hashanah lambs. The grain offering total for a single holy assembly day: one bull (three-tenths) + one ram (two-tenths) + seven lambs (seven-tenths) = 1.2 ephahs of fine flour, plus approximately two hins of oil and substantial wine. The material scale of major festival observance represented significant community investment, reinforcing that covenant worship was not abstract but economically substantial.
5: One male goat as sin offering for Rosh Hashanah. The sin offering at the New Year frames the beginning of the new annual cycle with explicit acknowledgment of communal failure: the year begins not with presumption of righteousness but with atonement. This theological structure — atonement before celebration — characterizes Israel's entire approach to covenant renewal; the year's proper beginning is forgiveness rather than accomplishment.
6: All Rosh Hashanah offerings are in addition to the monthly new moon offering and the regular daily tamid. On Rosh Hashanah, then, the total offering is: tamid (2 lambs) + new moon (2 bulls, 1 ram, 7 lambs, 1 goat) + holy assembly (1 bull, 1 ram, 7 lambs, 1 goat). The day that begins the new year receives the maximum cumulative burden — an expression of intensified covenant acknowledgment at the annual threshold.
7: The tenth day of the seventh month is Yom Kippur — wəʿinnîtem ʾet-nap̄šōtêkem — the day of self-affliction. The offering law here specifies only the additional communal sacrifices; the central atonement ceremony of Yom Kippur (the two goats, the high priest's entry into the holy of holies, the scapegoat) is legislated in Lev 16. Num 29 provides the supplementary burnt offerings that accompany the Lev 16 ceremony.
8: The Yom Kippur offering: one bull, one ram, seven yearling lambs — identical to Rosh Hashanah (v2). The structural match between the New Year and the Day of Atonement may reflect the ancient link between the beginning of the year and its most solemn covenant renewal. The Ten Days of Awe between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur in later tradition formalize this connection: the year begins with the call to repentance (trumpets) and culminates in its enactment (fasting, confession, atonement).
9: The grain offerings at Yom Kippur follow the same scale as all major holy assemblies. The liturgical uniformity of Yom Kippur's supplementary offerings with those of other major festivals asserts that the Day of Atonement is a festival like the others in its sacrificial structure, even though its ceremony (Lev 16) is uniquely solemn. Yom Kippur is not liturgically isolated; it participates in the same covenant-meal framework as all other appointed times.
10: One-tenth per lamb for the seven lambs of Yom Kippur — the same as all other holy assemblies. The uniform one-tenth-per-lamb formula creates a recognizable liturgical signature for major festival days, distinguishing them from ordinary new moons and from the extraordinary Sukkot bull-series that follows.
11: One male goat as sin offering for Yom Kippur — in addition to (milelāḏ) the Yom Kippur sin offering legislated elsewhere (Lev 16's two goats). This verse explicitly acknowledges that Num 29's goat does not replace Lev 16's elaborate goat ceremony but supplements it. Yom Kippur thus has three sin-offering components: the Lev 16 ceremony (scapegoat + offered goat for the people) plus the Num 29 sin-offering goat, making it the most richly sin-atoning day in the calendar.
12: The fifteenth day of the seventh month — Sukkot (Tabernacles) — begins with a miqrāʾ-qōḏeš and a seven-day festival with no regular work. Wəḥaggōtem ḥag laYHWH šiḇʿat yāmîm — you shall celebrate a festival to YHWH for seven days. Sukkot is the most elaborate festival in the sacrificial calendar, featuring the largest and most unusual offering series in all of Numbers: the decreasing-bull sacrifice from 13 to 7 over seven days plus an eighth-day closing assembly.
13: Day 1 of Sukkot: thirteen young bulls — the highest number of bulls in the entire calendar. The bull-count descends by one each day: 13, 12, 11, 10, 9, 8, 7 — a total of 70 bulls over the seven days. The Talmud (Sukkah 55b) interprets the 70 bulls as representing the 70 nations of the world (from Gen 10's table of nations), making Sukkot an act of global intercession: Israel sacrifices on behalf of all humanity during this festival. This interpretation gives Sukkot a universal, not merely national, theological significance.
14: The grain offering for Day 1 of Sukkot: three-tenths per bull (13 × three-tenths = 39/10 ephahs for the bulls alone) plus two-tenths per ram. The total grain offering for Sukkot's first day far exceeds any other festival day, reflecting the festival's extraordinary sacrificial scale. The precision of specifying three-tenths per each of the thirteen bulls — requiring a separate calculation for each animal — reflects the P tradition's mathematical thoroughness.
15: One-tenth per lamb for the fourteen lambs on Day 1 of Sukkot. Two rams plus fourteen lambs appear consistently across all seven days of Sukkot; only the bull count changes. The consistent ram-and-lamb complement against the decreasing bulls creates a mathematical pattern that was recognized and interpreted by later Jewish tradition as deliberately structured rather than arbitrary.
16: One male goat as sin offering on Day 1 of Sukkot — the same sin-offering goat present at every major festival occasion. The daily sin-offering goat throughout Sukkot's seven days means that each day of the festival includes both its own burnt-offering celebration and its own atonement component. The seven successive sin offerings ensure that the extended celebration of Sukkot does not accumulate unaddressed sin.
17: Day 2 of Sukkot: twelve bulls. The single-bull decrease from 13 to 12 begins the descending pattern that structures the entire festival's sacrificial logic. The symmetry of the descent — consistent single-unit reduction per day — creates a countdown toward the festival's end, each day slightly less elaborate than the previous. The descent from abundance to sufficiency mirrors the movement from harvest's plenty to covenant sufficiency.
18: Their grain and drink offerings for Day 2 are according to their number — the standard cross-reference formula used throughout Days 2-7 rather than restating every quantity. The formula kannišpāṭ — according to the ordinance/rule — establishes that the graduated grain and drink accompaniments of Num 15 govern all these offerings, creating a compact legal cross-reference system.
19: One goat as sin offering on Day 2 of Sukkot — the pattern holds daily. The repetition of the sin offering across all seven days of Sukkot has led scholars to read it as a daily renewal of atonement — not merely a single atoning act for the festival period but a daily covenant reset during the most elaborate festival of the year.
20: Day 3: eleven bulls. The decreasing series continues its mathematically regular pattern. The offering specifications for Days 3-7 (vv20-34) follow identical structural templates: N bulls + 2 rams + 14 lambs + grain offerings per number + 1 goat, all in addition to the regular tamid. The structural regularity makes the decreasing bull count the single variable that distinguishes each day from the others.
21: Grain and drink offerings according to the ordinance for Day 3's bulls, rams, and lambs. The kannišpāṭ formula efficiently covers the full graduated offering complement without restating what Numbers 15:1-12 established as the standing rule. The legal cross-reference system presupposes that users of the calendar have Numbers 15 available; Numbers is a cumulative legal document, not a standalone code.
22: One goat as sin offering on Day 3. The consistent presence of the daily goat sin offering throughout Sukkot maintains the dual character of the festival — celebration and atonement in tension. Israel's liturgical calendar refuses to separate joy from acknowledgment of failure; each day of the harvest celebration is also a day of covenant humility.
23: Day 4: ten bulls. The decreasing sequence continues through the middle days of the festival, each day's offering slightly diminished from the previous. By Day 4, the bull count has fallen to ten — exactly half the totals implied by the series' beginning and end. The mathematical pattern suggests intentional design: the festival moves from maximum sacrificial expression to minimum.
24: Grain and drink offerings per the standing ordinance for Day 4. The compact cross-reference formula is used for all middle days of Sukkot; the legislative efficiency reflects the priestly compiler's assumption that readers understand the system and need only the variable (bull count) specified.
25: One goat as sin offering on Day 4. The daily goat ensures that the festival's middle days, despite their less elaborate offerings, maintain the atonement dimension. No day of the seven passes without the offering that addresses sin — a structural assertion that sin does not take a holiday during Israel's most celebrated festival.
26: Day 5: nine bulls. The descent continues past the midpoint. Days 5-7 form the closing arc of the festival's offering series, moving from nine to seven. The final number seven — matching the number of days in the festival — creates a numeric culmination: the last day's bull count equals the festival's total length, a closure by coincidence or design.
27: Grain and drink offerings per the ordinance for Day 5. The standing-formula cross-reference is used consistently through the middle and late days, reserving full specification only for Days 1 and 8 (where the formulaic pattern changes), bracketing the full Sukkot specification with detailed introductory and closing statements.
28: One goat as sin offering on Day 5. The structural persistence of the daily goat through Days 2-7 makes the goat the unchanging element across the festival's variable bull series. Amid the decreasing bulls and the constant rams and lambs, the goat sin offering is the stable atonement anchor — the offering that does not diminish or escalate but remains consistently present.
29: Day 6: eight bulls. One day from the end of the main festival period, the bull count reaches eight — one unit above the final seven. The anticipation of the series' completion is felt in the offering itself; the penultimate day knows its position in the sequence.
30: Grain and drink offerings per the ordinance for Day 6. The formula holds through the festival's final full days, maintaining legislative consistency. The entire middle section of Sukkot's offering calendar (Days 2-7) is governed by the same kannišpāṭ formula, creating a block of structural regularity that frames the unique Day 1 and Day 8 entries.
31: One goat as sin offering on Day 6. The daily sin offering pattern is unbroken through the entire main festival period. Six straight days of daily atonement-plus-celebration makes Sukkot the most atonement-intensive period of the year outside of Yom Kippur itself — though each day's goat is a supplement to, not a replacement for, the major annual atonement of Yom Kippur ten days earlier.
32: Day 7 of Sukkot: seven bulls — the terminal point of the descending series. The coincidence of Day 7 and seven bulls gives the final day of the main festival a mathematical completion: the seventh day offers seven bulls. The total bull count for the seven days: 13+12+11+10+9+8+7 = 70. The interpretation of these 70 as representing the 70 nations (Talmud Sukkah 55b) understands Israel as completing a seven-day act of intercessory sacrifice for all humanity at harvest's climax.
33: Grain and drink offerings per the ordinance for Day 7. The formula continues through the final day of the main Sukkot period, maintaining the legislative uniformity that has governed Days 2-7. After Day 7's closing formula, the offering specification changes format for Day 8 — signaling the structural distinction between the seven-day festival proper and the closing assembly that follows.
34: One goat as sin offering on Day 7. The seventh and final sin offering of the main Sukkot period completes the week-long atonement companion to the harvest celebration. Seven days of celebration paired with seven acts of atonement gives Sukkot the most complete integration of joy and repentance in the entire festival calendar — and frames the eighth-day closing assembly as an emergence into renewed covenant relationship.
35: Day 8 of Sukkot: שְׁמִינִי עֲצֶרֶת (Shemini Atzeret) — the Eighth Day Closing Assembly. ʿAṣeret — assembly, closing gathering — marks the formal conclusion of the seven-day Sukkot festival. The Talmud (Sukkah 55b) interprets this day as YHWH detaining Israel for one more day: after sacrificing 70 bulls for the 70 nations, YHWH invites Israel to linger — just the two of them — for one more intimate day. The offering (one bull, one ram, seven lambs, one goat) is the smallest of all festival offerings, contrasting dramatically with Sukkot's opening thirteen-bull day.
36: The Shemini Atzeret offering: one bull, one ram, seven yearling lambs. The return to the one-bull format (matching Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur) after the elaborate seven-day Sukkot series creates a poignant simplicity: the festival that began with thirteen bulls ends with one. The single bull may also be read as a symbol of the unique divine-human relationship being celebrated: not the universal (70 nations) but the singular covenant between YHWH and Israel.
37: Grain and drink offerings for Shemini Atzeret per their number. The formula applies the standard graduated scale to the closing assembly's relatively modest offering complement. The small scale of the offering by comparison with the preceding days reflects the intimacy of the final assembly: this is not public celebration but covenant quiet.
38: One goat as sin offering on Shemini Atzeret, in addition to the regular tamid. The closing day of the entire festival season ends with the same sin-offering goat that accompanied every major occasion throughout the calendar. Sin-offering as the closing note of the festival year suggests that even at the height of covenant intimacy, Israel's atonement needs persist — a structural humility built into the calendar's final act.
39: ʾēlleh taʿăśû laYHWH bəmôwʿădêkem — these are what you shall offer to YHWH at your appointed times. The closing summary verse distinguishes these calendar-commanded offerings from voluntary ones: vow offerings (nəḏārêkem) and freewill offerings (nidḇōtêkem) are separate categories beyond the calendar requirements. The covenant calendar specifies the minimum communal worship obligation; individual devotion adds to it freely above that floor.
40: Moses told the Israelites everything YHWH commanded him — the compliance summary that closes the festival legislation of Numbers 28-29. The closing formula functions as a transitional bridge: Moses has transmitted the calendar instructions; Numbers 30-36 will move to vow law, Midianite war, land boundaries, and inheritance procedures. The festival calendar stands as the penultimate major legislative block of Numbers before Moses's farewell addresses in Deuteronomy.
